atom-fundamentals.expanded_explanation:
What is an atom? An atom is the smallest unit of an element that retains its chemical properties. Every atom has a nucleus at its centre — made up of protons and neutrons — with electrons occupying the space around it. A mental model
Mental model:
Think of the atom like a tiny solar system: the nucleus is the sun at the centre, and the electrons are planets orbiting around it — though in reality their paths are probabilistic clouds, not fixed rings.
Protons, neutrons, and electrons The atomic number (Z) is the number of protons in the nucleus and defines which element an atom is. The mass number (A) is the total count of protons plus neutrons. In a neutral atom, the number of electrons always equals the number of protons. Ions: atoms with a charge Atoms become ions when they gain or lose electrons. Cations are positively charged ions — formed when an atom loses electrons. Anions are negatively charged ions — formed when an atom gains electrons.
key_takeaways: The atomic number (Z) = number of protons; this defines the element.
The mass number (A) = protons + neutrons.
In a neutral atom, electrons = protons.
Cations are positive (lost electrons); anions are negative (gained electrons).
real_world_examples: Carbon has atomic number 6, meaning every carbon atom has exactly 6 protons. Change that number and you have a different element entirely — 7 protons makes nitrogen.
Sodium (Na) readily loses one electron to form Na⁺, a cation. This is why sodium reacts vigorously with water and why table salt (NaCl) is held together by the attraction between Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions.
Medical imaging relies on the behaviour of specific atoms — MRI machines, for example, exploit the magnetic properties of hydrogen nuclei (single protons) in the water molecules in your body.

what-are-social-sciences.subject: Social Sciences
what-are-social-sciences.expanded_explanation:
A definition that actually means something The social sciences are the disciplines that study human beings as social creatures — how we organise ourselves, how we make decisions, how we exercise power, how we build and break the systems we live inside. Where the natural sciences ask how the physical world works, the social sciences ask how the human world works. That includes everything from why people vote the way they do, to why some countries are rich and others poor, to why a particular street in a particular city becomes a neighbourhood people want to live in. The family of disciplines There is no single field called "social science." The term covers a family of disciplines that share a subject — human social life — but approach it from different angles. Political science studies power, governance, and the state. Economics studies how people and societies allocate scarce resources. Sociology studies the groups, institutions, and patterns that shape collective behaviour. Anthropology studies culture and what it means to be human across societies. Geography studies the relationship between people and place. International relations studies how states and global actors interact. Each of these has its own questions, methods, and traditions, but they overlap constantly — and the most interesting questions usually live in the overlap.
Mental model:
Think of the social sciences as different people studying the same object — society — but each using a different lens. The political scientist focuses on power and decision-making. The economist focuses on incentives and resource allocation. The sociologist focuses on groups, norms, and institutions. The anthropologist focuses on culture and meaning. The geographer focuses on space and place. None of them sees the full picture alone. Understanding society means learning to move between these perspectives.
What the social sciences are not The social sciences are not opinion or storytelling. They are evidence-based disciplines that use methods such as surveys, experiments, statistical analysis, archival research, and ethnography. However, unlike the natural sciences, their subject — human behaviour — can change in response to being studied. This makes prediction more complex and interpretation more important. Why this matters Almost every major decision in society — from taxation to education, healthcare, immigration, and climate policy — depends on assumptions about human behaviour. These assumptions come from social science, whether they are made explicit or not. Understanding social science helps you see how those assumptions shape decisions that affect everyone.
key_takeaways: The social sciences study human social life — how people organise, decide, and exercise power.
They are a family of disciplines (political science, economics, sociology, anthropology, geography, international relations) rather than a single field.
Each discipline offers a different lens on the same underlying object: human society.
Social science uses evidence and method, but its subject can change behaviour when observed — making prediction more complex and interpretation more important.
Almost every major public decision rests on assumptions drawn from social science, whether explicitly or not.
real_world_examples: When a government decides whether to raise income tax, an economist models the effect on consumption, a political scientist analyses the electoral consequences, and a sociologist studies how different income groups respond. The decision depends on all three.

Understanding why some neighbourhoods gentrify and others do not requires geography (location, transit access), economics (housing markets), and sociology (social networks, cultural signals) at the same time. No single discipline explains it.

Predicting how a country will respond to a climate disaster involves political science (state capacity), international relations (foreign aid), economics (resource allocation), and anthropology (cultural attitudes toward risk and authority).

why-social-sciences-matter.subject: Social Sciences

expanded_explanation.introduction: It is easy to take the social sciences for granted. Politics happens whether or not we study political science. People form communities whether or not we study sociology. Economies grow and collapse whether or not we study economics. So why bother formalising any of it into a discipline at all? The answer is that understanding why things happen is not the same as watching them happen. The social sciences give us the tools to move from observation to explanation, and from explanation to action.

section_1.heading: Making the Invisible Visible
section_1.content: Much of what shapes our lives is invisible in the sense that it operates through structures, norms, and systems rather than through obvious, direct force. Why do some people earn more than others? Why do revolutions happen when they do, and not before? Why do certain cultural practices persist across centuries while others vanish within a generation? These questions cannot be answered by instinct or casual observation alone. The social sciences build frameworks that make these hidden forces visible, nameable, and therefore something we can actually think about and respond to. Without that, we are left reacting to a world we do not understand.

section_2.heading: Turning Experience Into Knowledge
section_2.content: Everyone has personal experience of society. We have all felt unfairness, witnessed inequality, or noticed that the world does not quite work the way we were told it would. But personal experience, on its own, is limited. It is shaped by where we grew up, who raised us, what we were exposed to, and what we were never shown. The social sciences take that raw experience and place it inside a larger picture. They ask: is this pattern local or global? Is it new or historical? Is it accidental or structural? This does not make personal experience irrelevant. It makes it more powerful, because it connects individual lives to the forces that shaped them.

section_3.heading: The Foundation for Almost Every Major Decision
section_3.content: Social science knowledge underpins the decisions that affect entire populations. Public health campaigns are built on behavioural psychology and sociology. Economic policy is shaped by macroeconomic theory. International agreements rest on political science and international relations. Urban planning draws on geography and sociology together. None of these decisions are made in a vacuum, and none of them can be made well without the conceptual tools the social sciences provide. A society that does not invest in understanding itself is one that repeats avoidable mistakes.

section_4.heading: A Toolkit for Citizenship
section_4.content: Beyond policy and institutions, the social sciences matter at the individual level because they make better citizens. Understanding how political systems work makes it harder to be misled by them. Understanding how economies function makes it possible to evaluate competing claims about tax, trade, and welfare with real judgment rather than tribal loyalty. Understanding how societies change makes it possible to see beyond the present moment and recognise that what feels permanent rarely is. These are not abstract academic skills. They are practical tools for navigating a complex world with more clarity and less confusion.

mental_model.heading: Think of it like this
mental_model.content: Imagine you are standing in a city you have never visited. You can see the streets, the buildings, the people moving around you. But without a map, you do not know where anything leads, why the city was built this way, or how to get where you need to go. The social sciences are the map. They do not replace the experience of being in the city. They make it possible to move through it with intention rather than just wandering and hoping for the best.

key_takeaways: The social sciences make invisible structures and systems visible and understandable.
Personal experience is valuable but limited without the broader frameworks that social science provides.
Major policy decisions in health, economics, politics, and urban planning all depend on social science knowledge.
Understanding the social sciences builds the practical capacity to participate in society with greater clarity and judgment.
The goal is not detached academic knowledge but a sharper understanding of why the world works the way it does.

real_world_examples.title: Vaccine Hesitancy
real_world_examples.description: When vaccine hesitancy became a significant public health problem, the response could not be purely medical. Sociology helped explain how trust in institutions erodes. Psychology identified the cognitive patterns behind risk perception. Political science mapped how misinformation spreads through polarised communities. Addressing the problem required all three, which is precisely why the social sciences matter beyond the classroom.

real_world_examples.title: The 2008 Financial Crisis
real_world_examples.description: The 2008 financial crisis was not just an economic event. It was also a political failure, a regulatory failure, and a sociological event that reshaped how millions of people understood fairness and opportunity. Economics alone could not explain it fully. Political science, sociology, and history were all required to understand why it happened, why the response took the shape it did, and why its effects were distributed so unevenly across different populations.

real_world_examples.title: Migration and Integration
real_world_examples.description: When large numbers of people move across borders, the questions that follow are inherently social scientific. Geography shapes where people move and why. Economics shapes the labour market effects. Sociology examines how communities form and resist integration. Political science explains the policy responses and the backlash against them. No single discipline has the full picture, which is why the social sciences, understood together, are essential for making sense of one of the defining dynamics of the modern world.

interconnectedness-of-social-sciences.section_title: The Interconnectedness of the Social Sciences
interconnectedness-of-social-sciences.subject: Social Sciences

expanded_explanation.introduction: Section 1.1 introduced the social sciences as a family of disciplines — political science, economics, sociology, anthropology, geography, and international relations — each studying human society from a different angle. But this description risks leaving a misleading impression: that these disciplines are separate boxes, each sealed off from the others, each with its own questions and answers. In practice, the opposite is true. The social sciences are not just related. They are deeply interconnected and frequently overlap in practice. Understanding one often involves drawing on the others.

section_1.heading: The Artificial Borders Between Disciplines
section_1.content: Academic disciplines have borders because universities and publishers need them. They determine which department hires you, which journal publishes you, and which curriculum trains you. But the phenomena these disciplines study do not respect those borders. Poverty is simultaneously an economic condition, a political problem, a sociological pattern, and a geographical reality. A revolution involves the collapse of political authority, the breakdown of economic systems, the mobilisation of social groups, and the transformation of cultural meaning. No single discipline owns these phenomena. They simply exist in the world, and the disciplines are different instruments we use to study them — each capturing something real, none capturing everything.

section_2.heading: How the Disciplines Overlap
section_2.content: The overlaps between the social sciences are not accidental. They follow from the nature of what is being studied. Political science and economics share so much ground that the subfield of political economy exists precisely to study their intersection — how markets shape governments and governments shape markets. Sociology and anthropology emerged from the same questions about culture and collective life, and they remain in close dialogue. Geography informs both international relations (where territorial disputes and resource geography drive conflict) and economics (where location shapes productivity and trade). These overlaps mean that important research questions often encourage interdisciplinary work, and that productive conversations in the social sciences frequently happen across disciplinary lines.

section_3.heading: Interdisciplinarity as a Method, Not Just an Attitude
section_3.content: Saying that the social sciences are interconnected is not just a philosophical point. It has practical consequences for how good analysis is done. When a government wants to understand the effects of a new immigration policy, it cannot rely on economics alone — economists can model labour market impacts, but they cannot explain how communities form, how cultural integration works, or how political backlash develops. When a city wants to understand why crime rates differ across neighbourhoods, it cannot rely on sociology alone — geography explains spatial patterns, economics explains resource constraints, and political science explains how policing is organised and funded. Genuine interdisciplinarity means using each discipline's tools where they are most powerful, and knowing when to reach for a different one.

section_4.heading: Why This Matters for How You Study
section_4.content: If the social sciences are deeply interconnected, then the most important skill for anyone studying them is the ability to move between frameworks without losing analytical precision. This means being able to ask: what is the political dimension of this economic problem? What is the sociological context behind this political event? What does the geography tell us about this historical pattern? The sections that follow in this course will take each discipline seriously on its own terms — because the tools and traditions of each are genuinely distinct. But the goal throughout is to build a mind that holds multiple lenses at once, and knows which one to pick up in a given situation.

mental_model.heading: Think of it like this
mental_model.content: Imagine a team of doctors examining the same patient. The cardiologist looks at the heart, the neurologist looks at the brain, the orthopaedic surgeon looks at the skeleton. Each sees something real and important. But the patient is one person, and what happens in the heart affects the brain, and what happens in the brain affects how the patient moves. A good diagnosis requires the specialists to talk to each other. The social sciences work the same way. Each discipline is a specialist. The patient is society. Some of the strongest explanations emerge when specialists collaborate across disciplines.

key_takeaways: The borders between social science disciplines are partly organisational conveniences and partly genuine methodological distinctions — they reflect real differences in how each field approaches its subject, not just institutional history.
Major social phenomena — poverty, revolution, migration, conflict — are simultaneously political, economic, sociological, and geographical, and must be studied as such.
Overlapping subfields like political economy, economic sociology, and human geography exist precisely because the core disciplines need each other.
Genuine interdisciplinarity is a method: knowing which discipline's tools are most powerful for a given question, and combining them deliberately.
The goal of studying the social sciences is not to master one lens but to become fluent in several, and to know when to switch between them.

real_world_examples.title: The Opioid Crisis
real_world_examples.description: The opioid epidemic in the United States cannot be understood through any single discipline. Economics explains the pharmaceutical industry's incentives to aggressively market addictive painkillers and the role of patent law in pricing. Sociology explains how deindustrialisation and the erosion of working-class communities created conditions of despair that made addiction more likely. Political science explains the lobbying that weakened regulatory oversight. Geography maps the concentrated devastation in specific regions. Anthropology illuminates the cultural attitudes toward pain, medication, and healthcare-seeking behaviour. The crisis was produced by the intersection of all these forces — and any policy response that ignores any of them will fail.

real_world_examples.title: The Rise of China
real_world_examples.description: Understanding China's rise as a global power requires moving constantly between disciplines. International relations provides frameworks for how rising powers challenge existing hegemonies. Economics explains how export-led growth and currency management built the resource base for that rise. Political science analyses the one-party system that allowed long-term industrial planning without electoral short-termism. Sociology examines the social contract the state offered — rapid material improvement in exchange for limited political participation. Geography explains why access to maritime trade routes and border security with multiple neighbours shapes Chinese strategic priorities. None of these explanations is complete on its own. Together they begin to form one.

real_world_examples.title: Urban Inequality
real_world_examples.description: Why do some parts of a city thrive while others remain persistently poor, generation after generation? Geography provides the baseline — access to transport, proximity to employment, environmental quality. Economics explains how housing markets work and why property values compound advantage. Political science reveals how zoning laws, infrastructure investment decisions, and school funding formulas are made and who they serve. Sociology examines how residential segregation reproduces itself through social networks, hiring practices, and institutional trust. Anthropology asks what it means to grow up in a neighbourhood that the rest of the city has written off. The question seems local and concrete, but answering it requires the full range of the social sciences.

scientific-method-in-social-sciences.section_title: The Scientific Method in the Social Sciences
scientific-method-in-social-sciences.subject: Social Sciences

expanded_explanation.introduction: What does it mean to study human beings scientifically? The natural sciences offer a model: form a hypothesis, design an experiment, collect data, and revise the hypothesis in light of the evidence. The social sciences aspire to the same standard of rigour. But applying that standard to the study of human behaviour raises questions that do not arise when studying chemistry or physics. Humans think, react, and change their behaviour in response to being studied. Societies cannot be placed in controlled laboratory conditions. And the questions that drive social science research are often shaped by values and political commitments that natural scientists rarely have to navigate. Understanding what the scientific method means in the social sciences requires grappling with these complications — not as reasons to abandon rigour, but as reasons to think carefully about what rigour requires in this domain.

section_1.heading: The Empirical Commitment
section_1.content:
The social sciences share with the natural sciences a commitment to empiricism — the view that claims about the world should be grounded in observable evidence rather than intuition, tradition, or authority. This commitment shapes how social scientists collect data, construct arguments, and evaluate each other's work. A political scientist studying elections does not simply assert that economic conditions drive voting behaviour; they gather data on voting patterns and economic indicators, construct a testable hypothesis, and assess whether the evidence supports it. An economist studying the effects of a minimum wage increase does not argue from first principles alone; they examine what happened in places where the minimum wage was raised.

This empirical commitment distinguishes social science from journalism, philosophy, and political commentary, even when all four are addressing the same event or problem. Social scientists are bound by methods — ways of collecting and evaluating evidence — that constrain what they can claim and on what grounds. Those methods vary across disciplines and across research questions, but the underlying commitment to evidence-based reasoning is common to all of them.

section_2.heading: The Distinctive Challenges
section_2.content:
Applying the scientific method to human behaviour involves challenges that do not arise in the same way in the natural sciences, and social scientists have had to develop approaches that account for them.

The first challenge is control. In chemistry, you can hold all variables constant except one and observe the effect. In social science, that kind of experimental control is rarely possible and often not ethical. A researcher cannot randomly assign countries to different political systems and observe the results. This makes establishing causation — rather than mere correlation — harder in the social sciences than in the natural sciences.

The second challenge is reflexivity. When people know they are being studied, they often change their behaviour. This is not a methodological flaw to be corrected; it is a feature of studying conscious, social beings. A survey about attitudes toward immigration does not simply measure those attitudes — it may also influence them. A sociologist observing a community can alter its dynamics simply by being present. Social scientists try to account for this, but it cannot be fully eliminated.

The third challenge is values. The questions social scientists ask — about inequality, power, governance, and identity — are not value-neutral. Choosing what to study, what counts as evidence, and how to interpret results all involve judgments shaped by the researcher's own perspective. The natural sciences face versions of this too, but it is more acute in the social sciences, where the subject matter is directly political.

section_3.heading: The Range of Methods
section_3.content:
Because the classic controlled experiment is often unavailable, social scientists have developed a range of research approaches, each suited to different questions and contexts.

Randomised controlled trials — the gold standard in medicine — can sometimes be used in social science, particularly in economics and development research. A research team might randomly assign households or villages to receive a particular intervention and measure the outcomes against a control group. Where achievable, these designs can provide strong evidence of causation.

Where experiments are not possible, researchers use quasi-experimental methods. Natural experiments occur when some external event — a policy change, a natural disaster, a boundary drawn through a population — creates conditions that approximate experimental control without deliberate design. Comparative case studies examine different societies or time periods to identify patterns and test explanations. Longitudinal studies follow the same individuals or communities over time, allowing researchers to observe how variables change in relation to one another. Qualitative methods — interviews, ethnography, archival analysis — generate deep understanding of particular cases and contexts that statistical methods alone cannot capture.

No single method is appropriate for every question. Specialised depth within a single method can be as valuable as methodological breadth, and the choice of method should follow from the research question rather than from habit or fashion.

section_4.heading: What Social Science Can and Cannot Do
section_4.content:
A recurring question in the philosophy of social science is whether these disciplines can meet the standards that the term 'scientific' implies. The answer depends on what those standards are.

Social scientists can identify patterns, test hypotheses, build cumulative knowledge, and revise theories in light of evidence. In these respects, the social sciences function scientifically. What they cannot usually do is produce the precise, reliable predictions that physics or chemistry can. Human behaviour is shaped by many interacting variables, and societies change in ways that can alter the applicability of past findings. A model that accurately described economic behaviour in one era may perform less well in another, because the context has shifted.

This does not make the social sciences unscientific. It makes them sciences of a different kind — ones where explanation is often more achievable than prediction, and where understanding the mechanism behind an event can be more reliable than forecasting the next one. The goal is not certainty but well-grounded understanding, arrived at through methods that are transparent, replicable where possible, and open to revision when new evidence emerges.

mental_model.heading: Think of it like this
mental_model.content: Think of a detective working a case. The detective cannot rerun the crime under controlled conditions or observe it directly. They work with evidence that is partial, ambiguous, and sometimes contradictory — witness accounts, physical traces, timelines. They form a hypothesis about what happened, test it against the evidence, and revise it when new information appears. They cannot prove their conclusion the way a mathematician proves a theorem, but they can build a case that is more or less compelling depending on how well it fits the available evidence. Social scientists work in a similar way. They often cannot run the ideal experiment. They work with the evidence available, apply rigorous methods to evaluate it, and draw conclusions that remain provisional and open to revision as knowledge accumulates.

key_takeaways: The social sciences share the natural sciences' commitment to empiricism — claims should be grounded in observable evidence rather than intuition or authority.
Applying the scientific method to human behaviour involves genuine challenges: limited experimental control, reflexivity, and the role of values in shaping research questions.
Social scientists have developed a range of methods — including experiments, natural experiments, comparative studies, and qualitative research — to gather evidence under these constraints.
Explanation is often more achievable in the social sciences than precise prediction, because human behaviour is shaped by many interacting variables that change over time.
The scientific status of the social sciences rests not on matching the methods of physics or chemistry, but on the rigour, transparency, and revisability of the methods actually used.

real_world_examples.title: The Minimum Wage Natural Experiment
real_world_examples.description: In 1994, economists David Card and Alan Krueger studied the effect of a minimum wage increase in New Jersey by comparing employment in fast food restaurants there with neighbouring Pennsylvania, which had not raised its minimum wage. This natural experiment provided evidence that challenged the prevailing prediction that minimum wage increases would reduce employment. The study became influential not only for its findings but for demonstrating how a natural experiment could produce credible causal evidence in a field where controlled experiments are rarely feasible.

real_world_examples.title: The Milgram Obedience Studies
real_world_examples.description: Stanley Milgram's experiments in the 1960s investigated how far ordinary people would go in obeying authority figures, even when instructed to cause apparent harm to others. The studies produced widely cited findings about behaviour under authority and also prompted lasting debate about research ethics — the deception involved and the psychological distress caused to participants contributed to the development of formal ethics standards in social science research. The studies illustrate both what the scientific method can reveal about human behaviour and the ethical constraints that shape how that method can be applied.

real_world_examples.title: Longitudinal Studies of Education and Earnings
real_world_examples.description: Economists and sociologists have used decades-long longitudinal datasets to study the relationship between educational attainment and lifetime earnings, following individuals from childhood through adulthood and controlling for a range of background variables. The findings have informed policy on schooling, early intervention, and inequality. They also illustrate a characteristic limit of social science research: even well-designed studies identify average patterns across large populations, and those patterns do not translate into reliable predictions about any individual.

quantitative-vs-qualitative.subject: Social Sciences

expanded_explanation.introduction: Every social science research project must answer a fundamental methodological question: how will you collect and analyse your evidence? The two broad families of approach are quantitative and qualitative. Quantitative research works with numerical data — surveys, administrative records, structured observations — and uses statistical methods to identify patterns, test hypotheses, and measure relationships. Qualitative research works with non-numerical data — interviews, ethnographic observation, documents — and uses interpretive methods to understand meaning, context, and process. Both are rigorous when applied well, and both have genuine limitations. The question is not which approach is superior, but which is most appropriate for a given research question.

section_1.heading: Quantitative Research: Patterns and Measurement
section_1.content:
Quantitative research aims to measure social phenomena in ways that allow comparison, aggregation, and statistical testing. A researcher might survey ten thousand people about their political attitudes, record responses numerically, and use regression analysis to identify which variables predict voting behaviour. Another might use decades of economic data to test whether trade openness is associated with higher wages in developing countries. The strength of these approaches lies in their ability to identify patterns across large numbers of cases and to test whether those patterns are likely to be genuine rather than the result of chance.

Quantitative methods are particularly well suited to questions that ask: how common is this? How much does X vary with Y? Does this relationship hold across different contexts? They can produce findings that are, in principle, reproducible — another researcher using the same dataset and methods should arrive at similar results. This replicability is a significant advantage, and it has made quantitative research the dominant mode in some disciplines, particularly economics and political science.

The limitations are real, however. Quantitative research primarily measures what can be counted or systematically coded, and this shapes — and sometimes distorts — what gets studied. Poverty can be measured by income thresholds, but income does not capture everything poverty involves. Political participation can be measured by voting rates, but voting is only one form of participation. Statistical associations between variables do not automatically establish causation, and the design of survey instruments can influence the responses they elicit.

section_2.heading: Qualitative Research: Meaning and Mechanism
section_2.content:
Qualitative research works differently. Rather than seeking to measure and aggregate, it seeks to understand — how people make sense of their situation, why institutions work the way they do, what a particular historical event or social process meant to those who lived through it. A sociologist might spend a year in a factory, observing how workers negotiate their relationships with management and with each other. A political scientist might conduct in-depth interviews with senior officials to understand how foreign policy decisions are actually made, as distinct from how formal processes describe them.

The strength of qualitative approaches lies in their sensitivity to context and complexity. They can reveal mechanisms — the processes by which causes produce effects — that statistical analysis can identify but not explain. They can surface phenomena that researchers did not know to look for, generating hypotheses that quantitative work can later test at scale. They can access dimensions of social life — meaning, identity, experience, culture — that resist reduction to numbers.

The limitations are equally real. Findings from a small number of carefully studied cases may not generalise to broader populations. The researcher's presence in an ethnographic setting can affect what happens there. Interpretation of interviews or documents inevitably involves judgment, and two researchers working with the same material might reach different conclusions. These are not reasons to reject qualitative research, but they are reasons to be precise about what it can and cannot demonstrate.

section_3.heading: The Relationship Between the Two
section_3.content:
Quantitative and qualitative approaches are not in competition. They are suited to different kinds of questions, and the appropriate choice depends on what a researcher is trying to know.

Some questions are better suited to quantitative methods. What is the relationship between income inequality and health outcomes across a large number of countries? How has the gender wage gap changed over time? These questions require data that can be aggregated and compared statistically. Other questions are better suited to qualitative methods. How do people in a particular community understand the role of the state in their lives? Why did a specific policy fail despite strong political support? These questions require access to meaning, context, and process that numbers alone cannot readily provide.

Mixed-methods research — which combines both approaches within a single study — has grown in prominence across the social sciences. At its best, it can use quantitative findings to establish the scope of a pattern and qualitative work to explain the mechanism behind it, or use qualitative fieldwork to generate hypotheses that are then tested quantitatively at larger scale. Combining methods well, however, requires genuine competence in both and careful thought about how the two forms of evidence relate to each other. Mixed-methods work that is done carelessly can produce the weaknesses of both approaches without the strengths of either.

section_4.heading: Choosing the Right Tool
section_4.content:
The most important principle in choosing between quantitative and qualitative approaches is that the research question should determine the method, not the other way around. A common error is to choose the method that feels most familiar or most valued within a discipline, and then to find questions it can answer. This tends to produce research that is methodologically confident but intellectually narrow.

Different disciplines have strong methodological traditions that can make one approach feel like the default. Economics has historically favoured quantitative methods to a far greater degree than anthropology, which has favoured ethnographic and qualitative work. These traditions have produced genuine expertise, but they can also shape which questions get asked and what counts as an acceptable answer. Recognising this is part of developing a critical relationship with research.

The ability to evaluate work across methodological traditions — to assess whether a piece of quantitative research has adequately addressed its causal claims, or whether a qualitative study has been sufficiently rigorous in its interpretation — is a transferable skill that applies across the social sciences. No method is immune to poor execution, and familiarity with both makes it easier to recognise quality and weakness in either.

mental_model.heading: Think of it like this
mental_model.content: Think of the difference between a map and a conversation. A map of a city can show you where every street is, how populations are distributed across neighbourhoods, how far things are from each other, and how those patterns have changed over decades. It gives you coverage and comparison. A conversation with someone who has lived in the city for thirty years tells you something the map cannot: what it felt like to watch a neighbourhood change, why people moved where they did, what the local institutions meant to those who depended on them. You need the map to see the pattern. You need the conversation to understand what the pattern means. Quantitative research is the map. Qualitative research is the conversation. Many research questions benefit from both — though not always within the same study.

key_takeaways: Quantitative research uses numerical data and statistical methods to identify patterns across large numbers of cases and test whether relationships between variables are systematic.
Qualitative research uses non-numerical data and interpretive methods to understand meaning, context, and process in depth.
Both approaches are rigorous when applied well, and both have genuine limitations — quantitative research can miss meaning and mechanism, while qualitative findings may not generalise broadly.

The choice of method should follow from the research question: some questions are inherently quantitative, others inherently qualitative, and applying the wrong method to a question tends to produce weak research.
Different social science disciplines have strong methodological traditions, but awareness of both approaches supports a more critical and flexible engagement with research across the field.

real_world_examples.title: Measuring Poverty vs. Understanding It
real_world_examples.description: Quantitative research using income data and national surveys has produced important findings about the distribution and persistence of poverty across countries and over time. These datasets allow comparisons that would otherwise be impossible. Qualitative research — including ethnographic work by sociologists who spend extended periods in poor communities — has revealed dimensions of poverty that income measures miss: the social stigma, the cognitive burden of financial precarity, and the way institutions interact with people in poverty day-to-day. Both bodies of work have produced genuine knowledge; they are studying different aspects of the same phenomenon.

real_world_examples.title: Electoral Behaviour
real_world_examples.description: Large-scale survey research has identified robust statistical patterns in voting behaviour: income, education, age, and geography are all associated with political preferences in ways that can be measured and compared across elections and countries. But surveys are often less well suited to explaining why these patterns exist and what they mean to the individuals caught up in them. Qualitative interview studies and ethnographic work in communities have provided accounts of how people form their political identities and why they vote as they do — accounts that survey data alone cannot generate.

real_world_examples.title: Evaluating Development Programmes
real_world_examples.description: Randomised controlled trials have been used extensively in development economics to measure whether specific interventions — cash transfers, microfinance schemes, educational programmes — produce the outcomes they aim for. These trials can provide strong evidence of causal effects. What they typically cannot do is explain why an intervention worked in one context but not another, or what happened to participants beyond the measured outcomes. Qualitative case studies of the same programmes have often provided those explanations, and in doing so have shaped how subsequent quantitative studies are designed.

micro-intro.subject: Economics

expanded_explanation.introduction: Microeconomics is the branch of economics that studies how individuals, households, and firms make decisions about allocating scarce resources. While macroeconomics looks at the economy as a whole, microeconomics zooms in on the smallest units of economic activity — a single consumer choosing between products, a firm deciding how many workers to hire, or a market arriving at a price through the interaction of thousands of buyers and sellers.

section_1.heading: Scarcity: The Fundamental Problem
section_1.content: At the heart of microeconomics is a simple but profound idea: resources are scarce, but human wants are not. We cannot have everything we want, so we must choose. Every choice involves a trade-off — deciding to spend money on a holiday means not spending it on a new laptop. Economists call the value of the next-best forgone option the opportunity cost. Understanding opportunity cost is central to almost all economic reasoning.

section_2.heading: Incentives and Rational Actors
section_2.content: Microeconomics traditionally assumes that people respond to incentives and make broadly rational decisions — that is, they pursue their own goals in a consistent, purposeful way. This does not mean people are perfectly informed or purely selfish. It means that when the costs or benefits of an action change, behaviour tends to change too. Raise the price of petrol, and people drive less. Offer a tax rebate on solar panels, and more homeowners install them.

section_3.heading: Markets as Coordination Mechanisms
section_3.content: One of microeconomics' central insights is that markets — even without a central planner — can coordinate the decisions of millions of individuals through the price system. Prices signal information: a rising price tells producers to supply more and tells consumers to buy less, naturally adjusting the allocation of resources. This coordination is often remarkably efficient, though not always fair, and markets can fail in predictable ways that microeconomics helps us identify.

key_takeaways: Microeconomics studies decision-making by individuals, households, and firms.
Scarcity forces choices; opportunity cost is the value of what you give up.
Rational actors respond predictably to changes in incentives.
Prices coordinate economic activity without central direction.
Markets can be efficient but are also capable of systematic failure.

real_world_examples.title: Surge Pricing
real_world_examples.description: When demand for taxis spikes on New Year's Eve, Uber raises prices. This is microeconomics in action: higher prices incentivise more drivers to work and discourage some riders, restoring balance between supply and demand.

real_world_examples.title: The Opportunity Cost of University
real_world_examples.description: Choosing to study for four years isn't just about tuition fees — it's also the salary you forgo by not working full-time. Many economists argue this 'time cost' is the largest part of the true cost of higher education.

micro-markets.subject: Economics

expanded_explanation.introduction: Supply and demand is the foundational model of microeconomics. It explains how prices are determined in competitive markets, how markets respond to change, and why shortages and surpluses arise and resolve themselves. Almost every market analysis starts here — from the price of coffee to the wage rate of software engineers.

section_1.heading: Demand: What Buyers Want
section_1.content: The demand curve shows the relationship between price and the quantity consumers are willing and able to buy, holding everything else constant. The law of demand states that as price rises, quantity demanded falls — and vice versa. This inverse relationship exists because higher prices make a product less attractive relative to alternatives (the substitution effect) and reduce consumers' real purchasing power (the income effect). Demand shifts when something other than price changes: income, consumer tastes, the price of related goods, or expectations about the future.

section_2.heading: Supply: What Sellers Offer
section_2.content: The supply curve shows how much producers are willing and able to sell at each price. The law of supply states that higher prices incentivise greater production — more profit potential justifies using more resources. Supply shifts when production costs change (e.g., cheaper raw materials or new technology), when the number of producers changes, or when government policy (like taxes or subsidies) changes the economics of production.

section_3.heading: Equilibrium: Where They Meet
section_3.content: The market equilibrium is the price at which quantity supplied equals quantity demanded. At this price, there is no surplus (excess supply) or shortage (excess demand). Markets have a natural tendency toward equilibrium — if prices are too high, unsold goods accumulate and sellers cut prices; if too low, queues form and buyers bid prices up. When supply or demand shifts, the equilibrium price and quantity change, which is how markets continuously adapt to new information and conditions.

key_takeaways: Demand falls as price rises; supply rises as price rises.
Equilibrium is where supply equals demand — markets tend toward this point.
Shifts in demand or supply (not just price) move the equilibrium.
Prices act as signals, allocating resources without central coordination.
Government interventions like price controls can prevent markets from clearing.

real_world_examples.title: Coffee Prices and Weather
real_world_examples.description: A drought in Brazil — the world's largest coffee producer — destroys crops, shifting the supply curve left. With less coffee available at every price, the equilibrium price rises globally. Consumers feel this in their morning cup.

real_world_examples.title: Rent Controls in Major Cities
real_world_examples.description: When cities cap rents below market equilibrium, demand for housing exceeds supply. Landlords convert properties to other uses or stop maintaining them, and housing shortages deepen — a textbook example of a price ceiling's unintended consequences.

micro-consumer-theory.subject: Economics

expanded_explanation.introduction: Consumer theory is the microeconomic framework for understanding how individuals make choices when they face limited budgets. It asks: given what you want and what you can afford, how do you decide what to buy? The answers have implications far beyond shopping — they underpin everything from public health policy to the design of financial products.

section_1.heading: Preferences and Utility
section_1.content: Economists model consumer satisfaction through the concept of utility — a measure of the enjoyment or benefit a consumer derives from goods and services. The key insight is diminishing marginal utility: each additional unit of a good tends to bring less extra satisfaction than the previous one. The tenth slice of pizza brings far less joy than the first. This helps explain why consumers diversify — spreading spending across multiple goods rather than buying only their favourite thing.

section_2.heading: Budget Constraints
section_2.content: Preferences alone don't determine choices — consumers also face a budget constraint. They can only spend what they have. The budget line represents all the combinations of goods a consumer can afford at given prices. When prices change or income changes, the budget line shifts, pushing the consumer to a new optimum. A rise in the price of one good effectively makes the consumer poorer in terms of what they can afford, combining income and substitution effects.

section_3.heading: The Rational Consumer and Its Limits
section_3.content: Standard consumer theory assumes people make consistent, goal-directed choices — maximising utility given their constraints. This is a powerful simplification, but it is not always realistic. People have bounded rationality: limited information, limited time, and cognitive biases that lead to systematic departures from the optimum. Behavioural economics (covered in Topic 4) directly challenges and extends consumer theory by incorporating these psychological realities.

key_takeaways: Utility measures satisfaction; diminishing marginal utility explains why we diversify.
Budget constraints limit what consumers can actually choose.
Optimal consumer choice maximises utility subject to the budget constraint.
Price changes affect both what we can afford (income effect) and what we prefer (substitution effect).
Behavioural economics challenges the assumption of perfectly rational consumers.

real_world_examples.title: Supermarket Own-Brand Products
real_world_examples.description: When the price of branded cereal rises, many consumers switch to the own-brand equivalent. This substitution effect is consumer theory at work — the relative cost of the branded option has increased, making the alternative comparatively more attractive.

real_world_examples.title: All-You-Can-Eat Buffets
real_world_examples.description: Buffets exploit diminishing marginal utility. The first plate feels worth every penny; by the fourth, most diners stop — not because they're out of money, but because the marginal utility of another plate has fallen to near zero.

macro-intro.subject: Economics

expanded_explanation.introduction: Macroeconomics is the branch of economics that studies the behaviour and performance of an economy as a whole. Rather than asking why an individual consumer buys more coffee when the price falls, macroeconomics asks: why does an entire country's economy grow in some decades and stagnate in others? Why do millions of people lose their jobs at once during a recession? How does the money supply affect inflation? These questions require a different lens — one that looks at aggregate patterns rather than individual decisions.

section_1.heading: The Big Questions of Macroeconomics
section_1.content: Macroeconomics centres on three interrelated phenomena: output (how much an economy produces), employment (how many people are working), and the price level (how much things cost in general). Understanding how these three move together — and why they sometimes diverge — is the core challenge of macroeconomic analysis. A healthy economy typically exhibits stable growth, low unemployment, and stable (not zero) inflation. The task of policymakers is largely to keep these variables in a productive balance.

section_2.heading: Why Individual Behaviour Doesn't Simply Scale Up
section_2.content: One of the key insights of macroeconomics is that what is rational for an individual can be collectively destructive. During a recession, it is prudent for each household to save more and spend less. But if everyone does this simultaneously, total spending falls, businesses earn less, workers are laid off, and the recession deepens — a phenomenon Keynes called the 'paradox of thrift.' This is why macroeconomics cannot simply be derived from microeconomics: aggregate behaviour can be qualitatively different from individual behaviour.

section_3.heading: Schools of Macroeconomic Thought
section_3.content: Macroeconomics has been shaped by major intellectual disagreements about how economies work and what governments should do. Keynesian economics, developed after the Great Depression, argues that governments should actively manage demand through spending and taxation. Monetarism (associated with Milton Friedman) holds that controlling the money supply is the key lever. More recent schools, including New Keynesian and New Classical economics, have incorporated microeconomic foundations and expectations into macroeconomic models. These debates are not merely academic — they directly shape policy decisions affecting millions of lives.

key_takeaways: Macroeconomics studies whole economies: output, employment, and the price level.
Aggregate behaviour can differ fundamentally from individual behaviour.
The 'paradox of thrift' illustrates how individually rational actions can be collectively harmful.
Major schools of thought — Keynesian, monetarist, New Classical — disagree on how economies work.
Macroeconomic policy choices have direct consequences for living standards and employment.

real_world_examples.title: The 2008 Financial Crisis
real_world_examples.description: When US housing markets collapsed, the effects rippled through the entire global economy — banks failed, credit froze, businesses cut investment, and unemployment rose sharply. This was a macroeconomic event: a shock in one sector cascaded across the entire system in ways no individual actor could prevent or fully anticipate.

real_world_examples.title: Japan's Lost Decade
real_world_examples.description: After a financial bubble burst in 1991, Japan experienced a prolonged period of low growth and deflation. Despite low interest rates, spending remained weak — consumers and firms kept saving in anticipation of further price falls. This deflationary trap illustrated how macroeconomic expectations can become self-fulfilling.

macro-gdp-inflation.subject: Economics

expanded_explanation.introduction: To understand an economy's health, economists rely on three headline measures: GDP, inflation, and unemployment. These are not just statistics — they reflect the material conditions of millions of people. A rising GDP suggests more goods and services are being produced; rising inflation means the cost of living is increasing; rising unemployment means more people cannot find work. Together they form a dashboard for economic performance.

section_1.heading: GDP: Measuring Economic Output
section_1.content: Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is the total monetary value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given period. It is the most widely used measure of economic size and growth. GDP can be calculated three ways: by summing all spending (expenditure approach), all incomes earned (income approach), or all value added in production (output approach). All three should give the same answer. GDP growth above roughly 2–3% per year is typically considered healthy for advanced economies, though the 'right' rate depends on context. Critics note that GDP ignores inequality, environmental damage, and unpaid work.

section_2.heading: Inflation: The General Rise in Prices
section_2.content: Inflation is the sustained increase in the general price level across an economy. It is measured by tracking a 'basket' of goods and services over time. Moderate inflation (around 2%) is widely considered normal and even desirable — it encourages spending and investment over hoarding cash. High inflation erodes the real value of savings and wages, creating uncertainty. Deflation (falling prices) may sound appealing but can be equally damaging, as it encourages consumers to delay purchases, reducing demand and deepening recessions. Central banks typically target a low, stable inflation rate.

section_3.heading: Unemployment: More Than a Statistic
section_3.content: The unemployment rate measures the share of the labour force that is actively seeking work but cannot find it. Not all unemployment is the same: cyclical unemployment rises during recessions as demand for labour falls; structural unemployment results from mismatches between workers' skills and available jobs; frictional unemployment is the natural result of workers between jobs. Full employment does not mean zero unemployment — some frictional and structural unemployment is always present. The 'natural rate' of unemployment is the level consistent with stable inflation, and it varies across countries depending on labour market institutions and flexibility.

key_takeaways: GDP measures total economic output; growth signals expansion, contraction signals recession.
GDP ignores distribution, sustainability, and well-being — it is a measure of size, not quality.
Moderate inflation (around 2%) is generally healthy; high inflation and deflation are both problematic.
Unemployment has different causes — cyclical, structural, and frictional — requiring different policy responses.
These three indicators interact: low unemployment often pushes up inflation (the Phillips curve trade-off).

real_world_examples.title: Weimar Germany's Hyperinflation
real_world_examples.description: In 1923, Germany's inflation became so extreme that prices doubled every few days. Workers collected wages in wheelbarrows. The collapse in the real value of money destroyed savings, destabilised society, and left lasting political scars — illustrating the devastating human cost of extreme inflation.

real_world_examples.title: The Gig Economy and Unemployment Statistics
real_world_examples.description: Official unemployment figures can mask underemployment. Many gig economy workers are technically 'employed' but work far fewer hours than they want, at lower pay. This has prompted economists to develop broader measures of labour market slack beyond the headline unemployment rate.

macro-fiscal-monetary.subject: Economics

expanded_explanation.introduction: Governments and central banks have two main tools for steering the macroeconomy: fiscal policy (using taxation and spending) and monetary policy (controlling the money supply and interest rates). Both can be used to stimulate a sluggish economy or cool an overheating one, but they work through different channels, have different lags, and are governed by different institutions. Understanding them is essential for making sense of economic news.

section_1.heading: Fiscal Policy: Government Spending and Taxation
section_1.content: Fiscal policy refers to decisions about government spending and tax rates. Expansionary fiscal policy — increasing spending or cutting taxes — injects demand into the economy, potentially boosting output and employment. Contractionary fiscal policy — cutting spending or raising taxes — withdraws demand, useful when inflation is high. The challenge is timing: fiscal measures typically take months to pass through political processes and more months to affect the economy. A stimulus designed for a recession may arrive just as the economy is recovering, causing inflation instead.

section_2.heading: Monetary Policy: Interest Rates and the Money Supply
section_2.content: Monetary policy is set by central banks (like the European Central Bank or the US Federal Reserve). The primary tool is the interest rate: raising rates makes borrowing more expensive and saving more attractive, reducing spending and cooling inflation; cutting rates stimulates borrowing and investment. Central banks also use quantitative easing — purchasing financial assets to inject money directly into the financial system — when interest rates are already near zero. Because central banks are independent from governments in most democracies, monetary policy can be adjusted more quickly than fiscal policy.

section_3.heading: The Limitations and Trade-offs of Policy
section_3.content: Neither tool is a perfect solution. Fiscal stimulus can raise public debt to unsustainable levels. Monetary stimulus can inflate asset bubbles rather than reaching the real economy. Both operate with time lags that make precise calibration difficult. Moreover, the tools interact: aggressive monetary tightening can undermine the effects of fiscal expansion, or vice versa. The 2008 crisis and COVID-19 pandemic both required extraordinary coordination of fiscal and monetary policy, pushing both to their historical limits and generating ongoing debate about their effectiveness and risks.

key_takeaways: Fiscal policy uses government spending and taxes; monetary policy uses interest rates and money supply.
Expansionary policies stimulate demand; contractionary policies reduce it.
Fiscal policy is slower (political process); monetary policy is faster (central bank decisions).
Both tools have limitations: debt sustainability, asset bubbles, and uncertain time lags.
Coordination between fiscal and monetary authorities is crucial during major crises.

real_world_examples.title: The 2008 Stimulus Packages
real_world_examples.description: Following the financial crisis, governments worldwide launched large fiscal stimulus packages (the US passed an $800 billion package). Simultaneously, central banks cut interest rates to near zero. The combination helped prevent a repeat of the Great Depression, though it also left lasting questions about public debt and inequality.

real_world_examples.title: The ECB's Quantitative Easing
real_world_examples.description: When eurozone inflation fell dangerously low in 2015, the European Central Bank began purchasing hundreds of billions of euros in government bonds — 'printing money' to inject liquidity. This unprecedented monetary intervention remains controversial but is credited with preventing deflation and stabilising the eurozone.

dev-intro.subject: Economics

expanded_explanation.introduction: Development economics asks why some countries are rich and others are poor — and what can be done about it. It is one of the most politically charged and morally urgent branches of economics, dealing directly with questions of poverty, hunger, health, education, and human dignity. Unlike standard macroeconomics, which often assumes functioning institutions and relatively stable conditions, development economics must grapple with weak states, colonial legacies, geographic constraints, and the compounding effects of poverty itself.

section_1.heading: Measuring Development
section_1.content: GDP per capita is the most common starting point for comparing living standards across countries, but it is an incomplete measure. Two countries with the same GDP per capita may differ dramatically in life expectancy, literacy, or equality. The Human Development Index (HDI), developed by the United Nations, combines income with education and health indicators to give a richer picture of human well-being. More recent frameworks include measures of multidimensional poverty, which capture whether people lack access to nutrition, healthcare, education, and basic services — dimensions that a single income measure misses.

section_2.heading: Why Do Countries Differ?
section_2.content: The causes of diverging development paths are deeply contested. Geographic theories point to disease burdens, climate, and access to navigable waterways as constraints on growth. Institutional theories argue that secure property rights, rule of law, and inclusive political institutions are the decisive factors — countries with extractive institutions (designed to concentrate wealth rather than distribute it) tend to stagnate. Historical theories emphasise the lasting effects of colonialism: the extraction of resources, the distortion of institutions, and the drawing of arbitrary borders that cut across ethnic and linguistic communities.

section_3.heading: Growth vs. Development
section_3.content: Economic growth — an increase in GDP — is not the same as development. A country can grow rapidly while most of its population remains in poverty if the gains are captured by a narrow elite. Development requires that growth translates into improved living conditions for ordinary people: better health outcomes, access to education, reduced vulnerability to shocks. This distinction motivates a focus on the distribution of growth, not just its aggregate rate — and on building the human capabilities and social structures that allow people to live flourishing lives.

key_takeaways: Development economics studies the causes of poverty and the pathways out of it.
GDP per capita is a limited measure; the HDI and multidimensional poverty indices offer fuller pictures.
Explanations for underdevelopment include geography, institutions, and colonial history.
Growth is not the same as development — distribution and human well-being matter.
Development economics is inherently normative: it is concerned with what ought to change, not just what is.

real_world_examples.title: Botswana vs. Zimbabwe
real_world_examples.description: Two neighbouring southern African countries with similar colonial histories diverged dramatically after independence. Botswana built strong institutions, managed its diamond wealth prudently, and grew into a middle-income country. Zimbabwe pursued destructive land policies and collapsed into hyperinflation. The contrast illustrates the importance of institutions over geography.

real_world_examples.title: South Korea's Transformation
real_world_examples.description: In 1960, South Korea had a per capita income comparable to Ghana. By 2000, it was a high-income country with world-class technology companies. This transformation — driven by industrial policy, investment in education, and export-led growth — is one of the most studied cases in development economics.

dev-inequality.subject: Economics

expanded_explanation.introduction: Economic inequality — the gap in income and wealth between individuals, groups, and nations — is one of the defining challenges of our time. Inequality is not simply a moral problem; economists have shown it has concrete consequences for growth, social cohesion, health, and political stability. Understanding why inequality arises, how to measure it, and what effects it has is central to development economics and to broader questions of justice.

section_1.heading: Measuring Inequality
section_1.content: The most widely used measure of income inequality is the Gini coefficient, which ranges from 0 (perfect equality, everyone has the same income) to 1 (maximum inequality, one person has everything). Nordic countries typically have Gini coefficients around 0.25–0.30; the United States around 0.39; Brazil and South Africa above 0.50. Another approach is to look at income shares: what share of national income goes to the top 1%, top 10%, bottom 50%? Economists like Thomas Piketty have shown that in many rich countries, the share going to the top has grown dramatically since the 1980s.

section_2.heading: Causes of Rising Inequality
section_2.content: Multiple forces have driven rising inequality in recent decades. Technological change has increased demand for high-skilled workers and reduced demand for routine jobs, widening wage gaps. Globalisation has integrated labour markets globally, intensifying competition for lower-skilled work. The decline of trade unions has weakened workers' bargaining power. Tax policy changes — including lower top marginal rates and reduced taxation on capital — have allowed gains to accumulate at the top. These forces interact in complex ways, and their relative importance is disputed.

section_3.heading: Why Inequality Matters Beyond Fairness
section_3.content: High inequality is associated with worse outcomes across many dimensions. Societies with high inequality tend to have lower social mobility — the poor are less likely to escape poverty, and the rich are more likely to maintain their advantages. High inequality is linked to worse public health, higher crime, and lower trust. Politically, extreme inequality tends to undermine democratic institutions, as wealthy elites gain disproportionate influence over policy. Some economists also argue that high inequality reduces aggregate demand, slowing growth — since the wealthy save a higher proportion of their income than the poor.

key_takeaways: The Gini coefficient measures income inequality from 0 (equal) to 1 (maximally unequal).
Inequality has risen in many rich countries since the 1980s, driven by technology, globalisation, and policy.
High inequality reduces social mobility and intergenerational opportunity.
Inequality is correlated with worse health, trust, and political outcomes.
Addressing inequality involves trade-offs between redistribution, efficiency, and incentives.

real_world_examples.title: The Great Gatsby Curve
real_world_examples.description: Economist Miles Corak documented a pattern now known as the 'Great Gatsby Curve': countries with higher inequality also tend to have lower social mobility. In highly unequal societies, where you start in life strongly predicts where you end up — the 'rags to riches' story becomes less common.

real_world_examples.title: Nordic Equality
real_world_examples.description: Scandinavian countries combine relatively high equality with strong economic performance, challenging the assumption that equality and efficiency inevitably trade off. Strong unions, universal public services, and progressive taxation produce relatively compressed wage distributions without sacrificing innovation or productivity.

dev-global-poverty.subject: Economics

expanded_explanation.introduction: Extreme poverty — defined by the World Bank as living on less than $2.15 per day — has declined dramatically over the past two centuries, falling from affecting the vast majority of humanity to under 10% of the global population today. Yet nearly 700 million people still live in extreme poverty, concentrated in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. How do we understand the persistence of extreme poverty in an era of unprecedented global wealth, and what role can foreign aid play in addressing it?

section_1.heading: The Progress and Its Limits
section_1.content: The reduction of extreme poverty is one of history's most significant achievements, driven largely by economic growth — particularly in China and India. However, progress has been uneven. Much of the remaining extreme poverty is concentrated in fragile states affected by conflict, in landlocked regions with poor access to markets, and in communities facing multiple overlapping disadvantages. Progress has also been threatened by global shocks: the COVID-19 pandemic reversed years of poverty reduction gains, pushing an estimated 70–100 million people back into extreme poverty.

section_2.heading: Foreign Aid: The Debate
section_2.content: The role of foreign aid in development is intensely debated. Optimists like Jeffrey Sachs argue that targeted aid can break 'poverty traps' — situations where people are too poor to invest in their own futures. Sceptics like William Easterly counter that aid has largely failed because it bypasses local institutions, creates dependency, and is driven by donor interests rather than recipient needs. A third perspective, associated with Esther Duflo and Abhijit Banerjee, advocates for rigorous evaluation of specific aid programmes using randomised controlled trials to identify what actually works at the micro level.

section_3.heading: Beyond Aid: Trade, Institutions, and Migration
section_3.content: Most economists agree that aid alone cannot drive large-scale development. Trade — particularly fair and open access to rich-country markets — allows developing countries to grow through comparative advantage. Strong institutions (property rights, rule of law, absence of corruption) create the conditions for private investment and growth. Migration, particularly remittances sent home by workers who have moved to richer countries, now exceeds foreign aid flows as a source of income for many developing nations. Addressing global poverty requires a systemic rethinking of the rules governing international trade and finance, not just more aid.

key_takeaways: Extreme poverty has fallen dramatically but remains concentrated in sub-Saharan Africa and fragile states.
The aid debate pits optimists (Sachs), sceptics (Easterly), and empiricists (Duflo/Banerjee) against each other.
Randomised controlled trials have brought more rigour to evaluating what anti-poverty programmes actually work.
Trade, institutions, and remittances may matter more than aid for long-run development.
Global shocks like COVID-19 can reverse years of poverty reduction progress rapidly.

real_world_examples.title: The GiveDirectly Experiment
real_world_examples.description: GiveDirectly gives unconditional cash transfers directly to extremely poor households in Kenya and Uganda, bypassing traditional aid intermediaries. Studies show recipients use the money well — investing in assets, education, and health — challenging the assumption that poor people cannot be trusted to spend aid effectively.

real_world_examples.title: China's Poverty Reduction
real_world_examples.description: China lifted approximately 800 million people out of extreme poverty between 1978 and 2018 — the single largest poverty reduction in history. This was driven primarily by economic growth, not aid, though the state also made targeted investments in infrastructure, education, and rural development.

beh-intro.subject: Economics

expanded_explanation.introduction: Standard economics assumes that people are rational: they have consistent preferences, gather relevant information, and make decisions that maximise their well-being. Behavioural economics challenges this assumption by incorporating insights from psychology to show that real human decision-making systematically departs from the rational ideal. The result is a richer, more realistic picture of how people actually think, choose, and behave — with profound implications for policy, business, and ethics.

section_1.heading: The Origins: Kahneman and Tversky
section_1.content: Behavioural economics emerged from the work of psychologists Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky in the 1970s. Their research documented specific, predictable departures from rationality — patterns of bias that were systematic, not random. They showed, for instance, that people are loss averse: losses feel roughly twice as painful as equivalent gains feel pleasant. This asymmetry between gains and losses cannot be explained by standard utility theory. Kahneman later developed Dual Process Theory, distinguishing between fast, intuitive thinking ('System 1') and slow, deliberative reasoning ('System 2').

section_2.heading: Why It Matters
section_2.content: If people routinely behave irrationally in predictable ways, then policies and institutions designed assuming rationality will systematically fail. Pension systems designed on the assumption that people will voluntarily save for retirement find most people don't — even when it is clearly in their interest. Health systems designed assuming patients will follow medical advice find compliance rates are often low. Behavioural economics offers tools to redesign systems so they work with human psychology rather than against it.

section_3.heading: Behavioural Economics and Philosophy
section_3.content: Behavioural economics raises deep questions about autonomy and rationality that connect directly to philosophy. If our choices are systematically biased, are they truly our own? When a government uses knowledge of biases to 'nudge' people toward better decisions, is it respecting their autonomy or manipulating them? These questions sit at the intersection of economics, ethics, and political philosophy — which is precisely why the Economics × Philosophy case study is so rich with material.

key_takeaways: Behavioural economics integrates psychology into economic models of decision-making.
People are systematically biased, not randomly irrational — the patterns are predictable.
Loss aversion, present bias, and framing effects are among the most robust findings.
Dual Process Theory distinguishes fast intuitive thinking (System 1) from slow reasoning (System 2).
Behavioural economics raises philosophical questions about autonomy, manipulation, and what it means to choose freely.

real_world_examples.title: Auto-Enrolment Pensions
real_world_examples.description: When employers automatically enrol workers in pension schemes (with the option to opt out), participation rates jump dramatically — often from under 40% to over 90%. The decision to save hasn't changed; only the default has. This exploits status quo bias and has become a major tool of retirement policy worldwide.

real_world_examples.title: The Endowment Effect
real_world_examples.description: People consistently value objects more once they own them. In one famous experiment, participants randomly given a coffee mug demanded nearly twice as much to sell it as others were willing to pay. This 'endowment effect' — valuing things more once they're ours — cannot be explained by rational choice theory.

beh-biases.subject: Economics

expanded_explanation.introduction: Heuristics are mental shortcuts — quick rules of thumb that allow us to make decisions without exhaustive analysis. In many contexts they serve us well, but they also introduce systematic errors called cognitive biases. Behavioural economists have catalogued dozens of these biases, but several recur across virtually all human decision-making contexts. Understanding them is the first step toward designing better decisions and better systems.

section_1.heading: Anchoring and Framing
section_1.content: Anchoring is the tendency to rely too heavily on the first piece of information encountered when making a decision. In salary negotiations, whoever states a number first has a significant advantage — the other party's responses cluster around that anchor, even when it is arbitrary. Framing refers to how the presentation of information affects choices. People respond differently to 'a 90% survival rate' versus 'a 10% mortality rate' even though these are identical facts. Politicians, advertisers, and policy designers routinely exploit framing effects — sometimes to improve decisions, sometimes to manipulate.

section_2.heading: Present Bias and Hyperbolic Discounting
